title: 2026 04 23 Petitions Committee
description: Petition of Simon Palmer: Amendment to the Protection of Personal and Property Rights Act 1988 Public Trust NZ Glenys Talivai, Chief Executive Brad St Clair, General Manager, Legal & Governance NZ Law Commission Geof Shirtcliffe, Commissioner

chunks.text: Questions. Right. Now, so I'll introduce Greg O'Connor, chair of the committee. Greg Fleming from Maungakiekie. Pablo Garcia from New Lynn. Kia ora. I'm Cindy Wong Brown, Green List MP based in the Wairarapa. All right. So I think you've been here before, so we'll take your submission as read, but speak to it and just reiterate the main points. And, with both agencies here, we're sort of quite happy to merge, or not quite merge, but interact, shall we say, with both the- Right... submissions. So- Do you want me to sit there so-

Yeah, well come on up. Yeah. Yeah. Probably since you're by yourself. Have you got someone coming on Zoom, or it's just you? Just me. Good. Thank you. Well, tena tatou katoa. My name's Glamis Taylor. I'm the CEO of Public Trust. I've got Brad Sinclair with me, our general manager of legal and governance. We really appreciate the opportunity to appear before the committee today, and speak to our submission on the petition of Simon Palmer, which raises concerns about the cost of private manager examinations under what we

call the Triple PR Act in our business. I expect that the committee's also aware that Public Trust appeared before the Justice Committee earlier this month to talk on the same issue, in terms of our work under that same legislation. So I imagine that some of the issues will overlap with our discussion there.

At the outset, I do want to acknowledge that the personal and family circumstances described in the petition. We recognize the significant responsibilities carried by people who act as property managers, for loved ones who lack decision-making capacity. And we agree that the safeguarding arrangements under the Triple PR Act must operate in a way that's fair, proportionate, and focused on protecting the vulnerable people.

Our role in this framework is a statutory one. Under Section 46, we're required to independently examine the annual financial statements of court-appointed property managers, and report back to the court on our findings. We're the sole entity authorized to carry out that function.

And these examinations act as an important safeguard against financial mismanagement or abuse for some of New Zealand's most vulnerable citizens. So our submission really focused on four areas: our statutory role, the impact of recent regulatory and funding changes, the steps that we're taking to manage costs within our control, and potential options for Parliament's consideration.

As the committee will be aware, three changes came into effect in October 2024. That's the regulated hourly fee increased for the first time in 36 years. The asset and income thresholds that require a property manager were raised, and government funding that's previously subsidized examinations for low asset, low income cases was discontinued.

Now in principle, higher thresholds reduce unnecessary court involvement. The practical difficulty really arises for people already subject to existing court orders. Many likely qualify now for the lower touch property administration thresholds that have been put in place, but remain bound by appointments that generally last three to five years with no simple or low-cost pathway to make a change.

Now, this is what created what we have called a transitional group of people now facing examination costs. Often for the first time, despite having limited ability to pay. And Public Trust has estimated that this affects around 1,000 to 1,500 New Zealanders. And we are seeing in our work real distress, growing complaints, and increased debt where there's no realistic pathway for recovery.

Mm. Now, we're not a policy agency. We don't set the thresholds, the funding arrangements, or the regulated fee. And we don't have discretion to waive or reduce or alter the charges. However, we have consistently raised these transitional issues with officials and ministers, and we welcome the committee's scrutiny on how the framework is operating in practice.

We note that the matters raised by the petition sit within the broader context that the Law Commission's recent review of adult decision-making capacity law, which was reported to Parliament earlier in the year. Now that review signals a clear direction away from the highly prescriptive one size fits all arrangement toward a more flexible, proportional framework that reflects autonomy, dignity, and modern understandings of disability, while still maintaining the strong safeguards for vulnerable people. Now, we support this direction and

principle. Public Trust also welcomes any parliamentary consideration of legislation that is less prescriptive and allows greater flexibility in charging and oversight arrangements. This would better enable the fair and proportionate outcomes that we talked about earlier, especially for low risk transitional cases such as those raised in the petition.

Now, we're just happy to take any questions that the committee may have for us. All right. Well, thank you for that. And I appreciate you have been back and forth here. And of course, each of these committees, we sort of look at things through slightly different eyes. Well, not different eyes, but for different reasons- Yes... perhaps might be a better way of explaining it. So appreciate that.

Any questions before... We'll shortly, um- Yeah. Thank you very much for this. I knew nothing of this before the petition came before the committee, so I found it fascinating. I know, appreciate your caveat that you guys aren't policy, but it's just... So I'll try and keep my questions within the reality of that. Sure. So you say there's about 1,000 people affected by this. Is that right?

Yeah. We estimate about 1,000 to 1,500. Yeah, okay. And so even with the recently reviewed charges, once every 36 years, that's not frequent, is it? No. And I know, I did the prison value calculation and what it's gone to was very reasonable in my assessment. It literally is just bringing it back up to speed with where it was 36 years ago.

If you're talking, say, 1,000 people and it's what, on average working out to be about $700, is it? About three hours worth of work? Yeah, on average, a PME assessment takes around two to three hours. Right. So about $600 on average. Okay. So even at the upper end of your estimate, it's less than a million dollars total, isn't it?

That's correct. So it's somewhere between a half a million dollars, and the cost is a consequence of a court order for good reason. But yeah, I'm not usually the person on the committee to ask for the government to pick up a tab or something. But on this one, I'm looking at it going, that would be an interesting recommendation to consider.

So to be clear, we have highlighted to the Ministry of Justice that the transitional arrangements could be funded for just under a million dollars over a period of four years. So it's a total- A million dollars over four years... over four years- So that, and that, and then-... to allow the transition. And at the end of those four years, you'd then be down under the new thresholds.

How many people then would still fall? No, there would be no more transitional people because new people coming into the system- Would use the power of attorney... will be property administrators- Right... that would not need examinations. And equally, those then that may need to continue to have property manager, they would have to be over the raised thresholds- Yeah... where you would argue there is greater affordability.

Sure. Fair point. Do you have any idea of how many people would be at that level in terms of, because that's a subjective assessment in terms of relatives deciding whether that person is, whether it's really affordable to them or not. I know it's not, it's an objective test, but I can imagine in four years' time, people pushing back and going, "Well, it's still not very much money."

So during that period of transition, a couple of things will happen. One will be my existing property manager order will come up for renewal with the court. Yeah. And because I now sit under the new thresholds, I'd be able to apply to be a property administrator instead, which will alleviate the need for examinations.

Oh, is that right? So no one will- That's right. So you will not get examinations anymore because you're under that threshold. Okay. So literally in four years' time, no one will be having to get an order. No one will be caught with a property manager order who qualify for a property administration order. Yeah. Okay. No, I appreciate that. Sorry, I'm just trying to work out whether we can get any idea as to how many people are still going to be in that category in the new limit where they will still require an order.

So people who have over the new thresholds, which I believe are 25,000 for- Property... property. 30,000 for- For income... for income. Yeah. Those thresholds, the premise is, is that if I'm over those thresholds, I have a property manager order, and I pay for private manager examinations, recognizing that at that level, I've got greater ability to pay.

Mm. These transitional cases are ones that sit below those thresholds. Yes, no, I understand that, yeah. No, I was just trying to get an idea of how many are going to be in that new higher category in four years' time. But I guess it'll still be a few hundred people or whatever. And with our aging population and the increasing rates that we're seeing requiring property manager orders- Yeah... and it would depend on average incomes and what was happening.

Sure. Yeah. There's a number of economic factors that would play out. That'd be difficult to assess. Yeah. Okay. Thank you. Just but it's not the elderly, just the, it's also- That's right... disabled. My son's under one of these. That's right. Yes. So how does that determine, so I know you've got to conclude, but just one thing. No, I don't actually. Glenys answered it when her answer to Greg. Thank you.

Okay, and just to determine, how's the threshold determined? Like measured, not determined, measured. So at what stage, obviously income would be reasonably easy, but the property, because you're going to have to have a valuation anyway. How does that work? How do you know you've reached the- Yeah, so the income's relatively straightforward.

That's based on what comes in. The assets is more complicated, to be honest. It's based on a combination of factors, though. Financial statements from the subject person themselves, though also working with their court-appointed counsel to make sure that between all the various parties, so the court, Public Trust, and the counsel, we think we have a reasonable view- Right... of what those assets are.

I think we get it relatively close, but you are right, it is a complicated process. Yeah, because I just think of revaluations in that. Yeah. Particularly if that was a property. Now, although you're not going to get much for under that, I know. But yeah, I was just wondering how you reached, how it was determined that you actually all of a sudden go over the threshold.

And there is an issue, and this is why having future legislation that is less prescriptive may be helpful, is that there is also arguments in this space around what are liquid assets, right? So for example, with your KiwiSaver, if you're, as you pointed out, it's not always elderly people that are needing property orders or property managers. And if they have KiwiSaver and that's the asset, that's not a liquid asset that they can draw on to be able to pay for the fee.

And so- Sure... that is also a consideration that the legislation needs to take into account. Are you aware where MSD actually do pay these things on application, or obviously you have to be somehow be done? I'm not aware. All right. Well, thanks. Well, look, shall we hear from, is it Goff or Jeff?

Jeff. Jeff. Sorry, it wasn't- Jeff's spelled a confusing way. Yes. Well, that was why I had to ask. I felt the need to ask anyway. It's all right. Thanks. Mr. Shircliff, Commissioner, so we welcome to hear from you on the-From the Law Commission point of view. Thank you. Let me just start with a bit of context.

So, we're a statutory body, and our principal job is to make recommendations for law reform on areas of law that are referred to us by the minister. And once we've done that and published our modest reports, kind of the job's done. Is that an annual report?

No, no, that's the report on adult decision-making capacity- Oh, right. S**t... that's been referred to today by previous submitters. And I brought along just for a bit of context. So once we've done that, then our job is essentially done, and we don't have a role of ongoing advice. So, I'm limited to effectively what we did for this report.

And in this report, almost the first thing that we say is the Triple P Act, the act that we're currently talking about, needs to be replaced completely. So it needs to go. It needs to be replaced by modern legislation which has a focus on better compliance with the country's international human rights obligations, and a key focus on autonomy, dignity, and equality, and there's 175 separate recommendations for what that might mean.

Some of the recommendations we make is that the idea of a court-appointed property representative continue. So, there should be changes in the basis upon which they're appointed, there should be some changes around who is and is not suitable, and there should be some changes around how they go about making decisions on behalf of a person.

But importantly in this context, we also recommended that they be subject to an ongoing requirement to prepare annual reports and for those to be reviewed. Late in the piece, after our submissions had closed on our second issues paper, this issue came to our attention. But at that point, submissions had closed, so we haven't asked any questions about this.

And though it's obviously a significant matter, it was one amongst a huge range of matters that we were looking at. So, all we did in our report was note that the issue has come up, that we haven't looked at the issue under a particular act because we'd already formed the view that this current act needs to go.

But we thought it would be helpful to identify some of the things which would need to be considered in a new act, given that we had recommended that you should continue to have court-appointed representatives and the reasonable anticipation that the country will see more of them because however much we publicize the importance of enduring powers of attorney, we've got significantly increasing predicted rises of cognitive declines of dementias, and that will inevitably impose a greater requirement on courts for court-appointed representatives.

We also recommended that welfare representatives who look after a person's personal care and welfare should not have a compulsory reporting obligation, but that the court could order that they be so, so that there could be an additional requirement for some sort of review. So we thought that what we should do is say, in that new world, here are some things that would need to be thought about if there is going to be a fee charged against the assets of the people who are subject to court-appointed representatives.

And those things are not dissimilar to the things that would need to be thought about, that presumably were thought about under the current law, it's just we haven't thought about them in the context of the current law. And so those things are... The logic goes like this. Under Section 19 of the Bill of Rights Act, everybody has a right to freedom from discrimination on a prohibited ground, a prohibited ground defined in another act, but includes disability, and that includes intellectual and psychological impairment.

Discrimination for those purposes broadly means treating somebody differently on account of that prohibited ground in a way which results in material disadvantage to them. So the first thing we said was, well, it looks like there is a plausible case that the people who are subject to a court appointment are disabled and are treated differently to other people. And so in this context, the question is, the amounts you charge them, is that a material disadvantage to them? And that has to be viewed in context of the overall act, and it has to be viewed in context of the

way the particular fees are structured, the extent of any subsidy, whether that's subsidy rebates according to different levels of assets or income, et cetera. If the conclusion from that is that there is discrimination, then the next test is whether that discrimination is nonetheless justified, the discrimination in Bill of Rights terms being you have limited their right to freedom from discrimination because you're discriminating. The question then is can that be justified under Section 5 of the Bill of Rights Act?

And that broad test is under Section 5, whether the discrimination is a reasonable limit on the freedom from discrimination, demonstrably justified in a free and democratic society. And thatTest then the courts have interpreted broadly to mean, well, does the discrimination serve an important purpose? Which prima facie can justify it.

Is the way that the right to freedom from discrimination is restricted, is it rationally connected to the purpose? Is their right to freedom from discrimination limited no more than you reasonably need for that purpose? And is that limitation on the freedom from discrimination proportionate to the importance of the objective?

Those are not simple questions. They're really important questions. But they are the questions to which the answer must be yes, for any limitation on freedom from discrimination to be justified. Mm. And so all we could do was really say, given that we think there should be a continuation of reporting, that raises the question of continuation of who pays for that.

Mm. And it also raises the question, in the context of a new act, of those questions that you must answer. Now those questions are, as I said, the same questions that you would answer about the legislation today and the way the legislation pans out today.

But I'm sorry, I haven't done that analysis. All I can say is that's the analysis which needs to be done. Yeah. All right. Look, so because just as a petitions committee, what we endeavor to do is we're pretty try to eliminate party lines in here.

We're actually trying to come up as quite a unique committee, as any recommendations we do make, we want to make sure they're based on as many facts we can get. Yeah. And sending to the right place to hopefully get the right outcome. So feel free, if there's anyone else you think we could talk to that you're aware of, that might have another point of view on this, I just make that offer.

You may not immediately think of anyone because it's clearly, without it being a ... You're not a policy body, so you'll do what you have to do, and you'll be making recommendations. I was just wondering where the sort of slight gap between your two presentations, just wondering who would fill that gap.

The gap comes from the Ministry of Justice who was here before us. So I think you've already spoken to the people that we're talking to-... in terms of- That's-... transitional opportunities. That's the- We're also... And that connects back into the courts. We've also been working with the judicial body to improve the process.

Mm. And so that could be another avenue for you to talk to, is that judicial body. I'm sure they have a view in terms of some of the challenges that they might have in terms of even implementing the legislation through that level. Otherwise I would say, the Ministry of Justice is definitely the people that should be approaching.

And against the background, we know there's a lot of people who will exploit the situation for their own benefit. Mm. Whether it's, um- Could I just- I just experienced that... could I just add that, I think you're right to differentiate between people who are caught by this generally and this particular transitional group- Yes... who currently have property managers, even though if they were appointed today, they wouldn't need to be appointed.

They wouldn't, yes. Yeah. But our questions were directed generally to the question generally. So the question still needs to be answered in the affirmative, that someone who has say, $26,000 of assets, is it a proportional limitation on their right to freedom from discrimination to mandatorily require them to pay $750 a year, for example, for their accounts to be audited.

Which is the bottom line question, yeah. Yeah. And so that has to be answered even once you've worked through this transitional group. Mm. And it's just hard to see how you get around the need for notwithstanding the payment and who's paying. Mm. The need for, which is the whole basis of the Triple PR Act, isn't it?

Yeah. Yeah. And as we said, we think you need a new act quite badly. But that new act should continue to have the ability for courts to appoint property representatives, and a requirement for those property representatives to have to prepare reports and subject them to review.

The law should be about protecting and promoting the dignity, autonomy, and equality of people, and you can't do that without proper safeguards. Mm. All right. Any other questions? It's really a question, it's actually a question to discuss between the committee, but it's not a private question.

Oh, well wait, we'll discuss it afterwards. Okay. Yeah. But if there's anything we can give these people the experience- No, I think it's been covered. I mean, there's two issues, and we might not be able to deal with both of them, but we might. All right. All right. So the main thing I said is, the committee's now got a good understanding of how it all works, and I admit it, I've learned a bit more myself. Having been not a property manager, a welfare manager.

Mm. Now I know what the property manager does. Look, if there's no other questions from members, and nothing, no other suggestions you think we would go other than obviously Ministry of Justice. Policy work's being done anyway. Yeah. Great. Thanks very much.

Thank you. Thank you very much. I'm happy to leave you with a little light reading if you want to learn more about it. We've all done speed read- Let me know... we've all done speed reading courses, so even their-
